skill.name: dependency-auditor
skill.description: Audit project dependencies for known vulnerabilities, outdated packages, supply-chain issues, and license risks.
skill.summary: Find dependency security risks and produce prioritized remediation guidance.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command and network patterns, but review shows they are documented dependency-audit examples in SKILL.md rather than hidden executable code. No prompt injection, credential access, or exfiltration intent was found. The skill remains medium risk because it instructs agents to run package-manager, install, audit, and network-backed security tools that can change projects or contact external services.

medium_findings.title: Agent May Run Project-Mutating Package Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes commands such as audit fixes, package installs, uninstalls, updates, and CI hook setup. These are normal for dependency remediation, but an agent could modify dependencies or workflow files without sufficient user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly present and can alter a project. Confidence is below very high because they are documentation examples for a dependency-auditing skill, not concealed malicious automation.

medium_findings.title: Network-Backed Security Tools and Package Sources
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends tools and examples that may contact package registries, vulnerability databases, or third-party services. This is expected for dependency auditing but can disclose dependency metadata to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced tools commonly use network services, and the skill explicitly recommends Snyk monitoring. The risk is contextual because no hardcoded exfiltration endpoint or credential transmission was found.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives From Vulnerability Terminology
low_findings.description: Several high or critical static hits come from words in vulnerability examples, such as weak cryptography, network attack vectors, and SECURITY.md. They describe audit report content and do not access secrets or implement weak cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows these are explanatory markdown sections. I found no code that reads credential stores, implements cryptography, or bypasses security controls.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Example URLs Only
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs appear in documentation examples, including a migration guide and sample API calls. They are not used by executable code in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are inside markdown examples and comments. No evidence found that the skill sends project data to these URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples in Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: SKILL.md contains many shell command examples for audit, update, install, and monitoring workflows. These are useful but should be executed only after the user approves the project and command scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are direct evidence of external command usage. They are aligned with the skill purpose, so the pattern is risky operationally rather than clearly malicious.

content.value_statement: Dependency vulnerabilities and outdated packages create release risk and hidden exposure. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code inspect dependency files and produce prioritized remediation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Reviews common dependency manifests such as package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod, Gemfile, and Maven files.
Classifies dependency findings by severity and remediation priority.
Suggests safer package versions, replacement packages, and migration steps.
Highlights supply-chain concerns such as deprecated packages, typosquatting, and maintainer risk.
Checks license compatibility concerns and flags packages that need legal review.
Formats findings as an audit report with action items and verification steps.
limitations: Requires access to project dependency files and lock files for accurate results.
May rely on external audit tools or vulnerability databases for current CVE data.
Does not guarantee that every transitive dependency risk will be detected.
Remediation commands can change project files and should be reviewed before execution.

use_cases.title: Pre-release Dependency Review
use_cases.description: Audit dependency files before a release and identify updates that should block deployment.

use_cases.title: Supply Chain Triage
use_cases.description: Prioritize vulnerable, deprecated, or suspicious packages across a repository.

use_cases.title: License and Maintenance Check
use_cases.description: Find packages that create license, maintenance, or long-term support concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Dependency Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use dependency-auditor to review this project for known dependency vulnerabilities. Summarize the highest priority fixes first.

prompt_templates.title: Audit With Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the dependency files and create a remediation plan. Include fixed versions, expected breaking changes, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Supply Chain and License Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review dependencies for vulnerabilities, deprecated packages, suspicious packages, and license concerns. Separate security risks from legal review items.

prompt_templates.title: CI Gate Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a dependency security gate for this repository. Recommend audit commands, severity thresholds, false-positive handling, and developer workflow changes.

output_examples.input: Audit a Node.js application before release.
output_examples.output: A prioritized report listing critical and high dependency risks, affected packages, fixed versions, and release-blocking recommendations.

output_examples.input: Review Python requirements for vulnerable packages.
output_examples.output: A concise vulnerability summary with package names, severity, upgrade targets, and test areas to verify after updates.
A risk review covering deprecated packages, suspicious names, maintainer concerns, license issues, and recommended replacements.

best_practices: Run audits on both manifest files and lock files so direct and transitive dependencies are covered.
Review proposed update commands before execution, especially fixes that change major versions.
Test dependency updates in a branch or staging environment before merging or deploying.
anti_patterns: Running automatic fixes without reading breaking-change notes or testing the application.
Ignoring low severity findings forever instead of tracking them through normal maintenance.
Adding new dependencies without checking maintenance status, license, and transitive package impact.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically fix vulnerable dependencies?
faq.answer: It can suggest fix commands, but users should approve and test changes before applying them.

faq.question: Which ecosystems does it cover?
faq.answer: It documents workflows for Node.js, Python, Go, Ruby, Java, Maven, and .NET dependency audits.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: Accurate CVE checks often need package registries or vulnerability databases. Offline review may be incomplete.

faq.question: Can it review license risk?
faq.answer: Yes. It can flag license compatibility concerns and identify packages that need legal review.

faq.question: Can it find typosquatting or suspicious packages?
faq.answer: It includes supply-chain review guidance for suspicious packages, maintainers, deprecation, and package integrity.

faq.question: Is every reported CVE automatically exploitable?
faq.answer: No. The report should include exploitability and usage context so teams can prioritize accurately.
